title: The Steppe After the Scythians
bullets: Around 300 BCE the Scythians were fading on the western steppe while the Mongolian steppe was producing a new kind of nomadic power that would force both China and Rome to reorganize.
The Mongolian steppe was the harshest stretch of the Eurasian grassland belt, with winters hitting minus 40 degrees and nomads herding sheep, goats, cattle, and horses across seasonal circuits.
During the Warring States period the northern Chinese kingdoms of Zhao, Yan, and Qin each built rammed-earth walls along their frontiers to block raids by the Donghu, Yuezhi, and proto-Xiongnu.

In 221 BCE Qin Shi Huang unified China, and in 215 BCE his general Meng Tian drove the Xiongnu north of the Yellow River bend with 300,000 troops and connected the existing walls into the first Great Wall.

The Great Wall cut the nomads off from Chinese iron, grain, silk, and manufactured goods they needed to survive, creating an existential crisis that would force the Xiongnu to organize at unprecedented scale.
Touman was chanyu in the late 200s BCE, but he sent his eldest son Modu as a hostage to the Yuezhi and then attacked them, hoping they would kill the boy in retaliation.
Sima Qian, writing around 100 BCE, devoted an entire chapter of his Shiji to the Xiongnu and is virtually the only source for their early history since the Xiongnu left no written records.
The Xiongnu name first appears in Chinese records around 318 BCE, and the relationship between them and earlier nomadic groups mentioned in Chinese texts remains uncertain.

title: Modu Chanyu
bullets: In 209 BCE Modu killed his father Touman after training his elite guard with whistling arrows, executing any warrior who hesitated to shoot at his favorite horse, his wife, and finally his father.
Modu destroyed the Donghu confederation to the east by feigning weakness, giving away his best horses and favorite wife before launching a surprise attack that crushed them completely.
Modu drove the Yuezhi westward from the Gansu Corridor, triggering a migration that eventually took them to Bactria where they founded the Kushan Empire centuries later.
By roughly 203 BCE Modu controlled territory from Manchuria to the Altai Mountains, absorbing the Loulan, Wusun, Qiang, and Siberian forest peoples into the largest steppe empire before the Mongols.
The Xiongnu political system divided the empire into left and right wings under wise kings, with a decimal military organization of 10,000 down to 10 that doubled as the entire state administration.
Each autumn the Xiongnu gathered at Longcheng for an annual assembly where the chanyu conducted sacrifices to Tengri the sky god, reviewed troops, and redistributed wealth.
The chanyu claimed the title Son of Heaven, deliberately matching the Chinese emperor's cosmic claim and asserting equal divine right to rule, which neither side recognized in the other.
Modu's rise in 209 BCE coincided with the Qin dynasty's collapse, the Second Punic War in the Mediterranean, and the Maurya Empire's height under Ashoka in India.

title: The Humiliation at Baideng
bullets: In 200 BCE Modu trapped Emperor Gaozu and his army on Mount Baideng for seven days by hiding 400,000 cavalry behind the surrounding hills and sorting them by horse color on each flank.
Gaozu escaped only after his advisor Chen Ping bribed Modu's wife by warning that beautiful Chinese tribute women would replace her, and she persuaded Modu to open a gap in the siege lines.
The 198 BCE heqin treaty forced China to send an imperial princess, annual shipments of silk, grain, and wine, and acknowledge the Xiongnu chanyu as an equal to the Chinese emperor.
In 192 BCE Modu sent Empress Lu a deliberately crude marriage proposal; she swallowed the insult and renewed the heqin treaty because the Han military had not improved since Baideng.
Xiongnu traded Chinese silk westward through Central Asian oasis towns, inadvertently financing the proto-Silk Road that connected China to Persian and eventually Roman markets.
Modu died around 174 BCE after a thirty-five year reign; his son Laoshang finished off the Yuezhi by killing their king and turning his skull into a drinking cup.
Under Laoshang's successor Junchen, a Xiongnu raiding force in 166 BCE penetrated so deep into Chinese territory that it came within sight of the Han capital Chang'an.
The heqin era overlaps with Rome's destruction of Carthage in 146 BCE and the Seleucid Empire's decline, making the Han-Xiongnu tribute relationship unique in the ancient world.

title: Emperor Wu Strikes Back
bullets: Emperor Wu took the throne at fifteen in 141 BCE determined to end the sixty-year heqin system, launching the most militarily aggressive era in Chinese history before the modern period.
In 138 BCE Wu sent Zhang Qian to find the Yuezhi for an anti-Xiongnu alliance, but the Xiongnu captured him for ten years; his failed mission produced intelligence that opened the Silk Road.
The 133 BCE Mayi ambush was a complete fiasco when the chanyu discovered 300,000 hidden troops and withdrew untouched, but it ended heqin and started decades of open war.
General Wei Qing, the son of a palace servant, recaptured the Ordos Loop starting in 129 BCE and proved that retrained Chinese cavalry could defeat Xiongnu forces in open battle.
The teenage prodigy Huo Qubing seized the Hexi Corridor in 121 BCE at age twenty, capturing the golden idol of Xiongnu sky worship and cutting the Xiongnu off from Central Asian trade routes.
The 119 BCE Battle of Mobei sent 100,000 Chinese cavalry north of the Gobi in a two-pronged invasion that drove the chanyu from his base camp and killed or captured over 70,000 Xiongnu.
Huo Qubing died at 24 from unknown causes; Emperor Wu built him a tomb shaped like the Qilian Mountains and he remains one of the most celebrated military figures in Chinese history.
In 99 BCE the young general Li Ling fought 80,000 Xiongnu cavalry with only 5,000 infantry for a week before being captured, and Sima Qian was castrated for defending him at court.
The wars opened the Silk Road by roughly 115 BCE, connecting Chinese silk and lacquerware to Central Asian horses, jade, Mediterranean glass, and Roman gold along territory Huo Qubing had cleared.

title: The Split
bullets: Around 60 BCE the Xiongnu succession system collapsed with five claimants fighting simultaneously for the chanyu title, shattering the confederacy into factions that never fully reunited.
In 51 BCE Huhanye Chanyu did the unthinkable: he traveled to the Han court and prostrated himself before the Chinese emperor, reversing the power dynamic Modu had established at Baideng.
In 36 BCE Han general Chen Tang marched west and destroyed Zhizhi Chanyu's stronghold near the Talas River in modern Kazakhstan, the westernmost military action of the Han dynasty.
In 33 BCE the court lady Wang Zhaojun was sent as bride to Huhanye Chanyu; her story of sacrifice and exile became one of the most celebrated tales in two thousand years of Chinese literature.
The formal split came in 48 CE when the Southern Xiongnu submitted as Han frontier guards in the Ordos while the Northern Xiongnu maintained independence on the Mongolian steppe.
Han general Ban Chao spent thirty years from 73 CE pacifying the Western Regions, cutting Northern Xiongnu access to Silk Road trade and tightening the strategic noose.
In 89 CE a combined Han-Southern Xiongnu force crushed the Northern Xiongnu at the Battle of Ikh Bayan, killing or capturing over 200,000 and shattering their power.
In 93 CE the Northern Xiongnu remnants fled west beyond the Altai Mountains and vanished from Chinese records, opening the great mystery of whether they became the Huns.
In 304 CE the Sinicized Xiongnu aristocrat Liu Yuan declared himself emperor, and his successor Liu Cong sacked the Western Jin capital Luoyang in 311 CE, taking the emperor prisoner.

title: The Westward Migration
bullets: A 300-year gap separates the Northern Xiongnu vanishing from Chinese records around 93 CE and the Huns appearing north of the Black Sea around 370 CE, and no source tracks the migration.

The case for Xiongnu-Hun connection rests on the phonetic similarity of the names, westward direction, identical mounted archery tactics, intermediate peoples called Xionites, and similar bronze cauldrons.

The case against includes the 300-year transformation gap, near-total absence of linguistic evidence, the multiethnic nature of both confederacies, and genuine scholarly disagreement spanning a century.
Around 370 CE the Huns crushed the Alans north of the Black Sea, then destroyed the Ostrogothic kingdom of Ermanaric, who reportedly killed himself rather than face them.

In 376 CE the terrified Visigoths begged Emperor Valens to cross the Danube as refugees; Roman officials exploited them, they revolted, and in 378 destroyed a Roman army at Adrianople, killing Valens.
Ammianus Marcellinus described the Huns as barely human: scarified faces, raw meat softened under saddles, no farming, no dwellings, and terrifying composite bow tactics identical to the Xiongnu's.
The Huns practiced artificial cranial deformation, binding infant heads to produce elongated skulls, a dramatic modification that spread to subject peoples and horrified Roman observers.
Recent ancient DNA studies found East Asian genetic components in some Hunnic-era burials in Central Europe, consistent with but not proof of Mongolian steppe origins.

title: Attila
bullets: In 434 CE brothers Attila and Bleda inherited the Hunnic empire from their uncle Rua, who had already forced Constantinople to pay 350 pounds of gold annually in tribute.
The 435 Treaty of Margus doubled Constantinople's tribute to 700 pounds of gold and required the return of all Hunnic fugitives, some of whom had been baptized as Roman citizens.
Around 445 Attila murdered Bleda during a hunting trip and became sole ruler, paralleling Modu's patricide six centuries earlier in what the steppe tradition normalized as the cost of power.
Byzantine diplomat Priscus visited Attila's wooden palace in 449 and described him eating plain food from a wooden plate while guests feasted on gold, a calculated performance of austere power.
In 441 Attila devastated the Balkans, destroying Singidunum, Sirmium, and Naissus, advancing within 20 miles of Constantinople before the Theodosian Walls stopped his cavalry.
The 447 Treaty of Anatolius tripled tribute to 2,100 pounds of gold annually with a 6,000-pound back-payment, making Constantinople the world's richest city paying protection money.
In 451 Attila invaded Gaul and was stopped at the Battle of the Catalaunian Plains by Aetius and the Visigoths, the first time his army had been halted in open battle.
In 452 Attila sacked Aquileia, Milan, and other Italian cities; refugees fleeing to marshy lagoons founded settlements that would become Venice.
Pope Leo I met Attila at the Mincio River and he withdrew, probably due to plague, stretched supply lines, and Eastern Roman reinforcements rather than papal authority alone.
In 453 Attila died on his wedding night to the Gothic bride Ildico, drowning in blood from a nosebleed; his warriors slashed their faces so the greatest warrior would be wept in blood.

title: After Attila
bullets: Attila's sons Ellac, Dengizich, and Ernak quarreled over the succession immediately, repeating the exact pattern that had destroyed the Xiongnu after their succession crises of the 60s BCE.
In 454 CE the Gepid king Ardaric led a coalition of Ostrogoths, Heruli, Rugians, and Scirians that destroyed the Huns at the Battle of Nedao, killing Ellac and shattering Hunnic power.
In 469 CE Dengizich was killed fighting Roman forces, and his head was displayed on a pole in Constantinople's Hippodrome, the final act of the Hunnic military threat to Europe.
Ernak accepted subordination to Constantinople and settled his followers in the lower Danube region, where they merged with Avars, Bulgars, and Slavs until the name Hun ceased to exist.
The Huns triggered the Volkerwanderung that sent Goths, Vandals, Burgundians, and Franks crashing through the Roman frontier, reshaping Europe into the medieval kingdoms that followed.
Every subsequent steppe power from the Avars to the Magyars settled the same Hungarian plain, used the same military techniques, and followed the template Modu had invented in 209 BCE.
Modu built the blueprint that every steppe empire followed for fifteen centuries: decimal military organization, dual-wing administration, annual assembly, and tributary extortion of settled empires.
The settled world's answer was always the same: walls, tribute, and eventually absorption, but absorption took centuries, and the nomads reshaped every civilization they touched.
